note: 20 new Qs to append to v2.json. UI track focus + Mock Interview senior-UI behavioral.

questions.question: You inherit a codebase where third-party CSS keeps fighting your utility classes with high-specificity selectors. Which approach gives the most predictable cascade control without rewriting third-party styles?

options.text: Wrap third-party styles in @layer vendor and your own utilities in @layer utilities — define the layer order so utilities always win.
Add !important to every utility class.
Boost specificity by chaining your class selectors three times (.btn.btn.btn).
Switch the whole project to CSS-in-JS.

explanations.a: Correct. @layer flattens specificity within layers and lets you declare an explicit order at the top — `@layer reset, vendor, base, components, utilities;`. Utilities win regardless of specificity inside them.
explanations.b: Wrong. !important creates the same arms race one level up, and !important inside layers behaves inversely (earlier layers win for !important).
explanations.c: Brittle and signals a deeper architectural problem. Specificity wars don't scale.
explanations.d: Possible but a sledgehammer — @layer solves the exact problem with no migration.

questions.question: Your design system has ~120 components. The team is split on methodology: pure BEM, utility-first (Tailwind-like), or CUBE CSS. Which choice is most defensible for a primitive + composite component library that other teams consume?

options.text: Tokens-first + utilities for primitives, semantic class names for composite components — the methodology serves the layer, not the other way around.
Pure BEM everywhere — most consistent.
Tailwind for everything; tokens live in tailwind.config.ts.
CUBE CSS for everything because it's newer.

explanations.a: Correct. Mature design systems mix: tokens are the contract, utilities express primitives cheaply, composite components get readable semantic classes for clarity at the boundary. Methodology is a means to an end.
explanations.b: Pure BEM scales poorly for primitives (lots of single-prop classes) and obscures composition.
explanations.c: Utilities-only works in apps; component-library consumers want a stable API, not a wall of utilities they have to reproduce.
explanations.d: Wrong. Choose methodologies for fit, not novelty.

questions.question: You want a utility class .text-center to always win over component-level styles, without using !important. Best technique?

options.text: Wrap component styles in :where(.component {...}) so they have zero specificity; utilities sit above them naturally.
Increase utility specificity by ID selectors (#text-center).
Put utilities in a <style> tag that loads last in the head.
Add data attributes and target those instead of classes.

explanations.a: Correct. :where() has zero specificity by spec — wrapping component selectors in it lets normal class-specificity utilities override them cleanly. Pairs beautifully with @layer.
explanations.b: Wrong. Promoting to IDs is a one-off win that breaks the moment another author does the same.
explanations.c: Source order is overridden by specificity. Specificity wins over order.
explanations.d: Data attributes don't have lower specificity than classes — same level.

questions.question: Design: name the layers in your CSS architecture for a multi-team monorepo where 8 product apps share a single design system package. What goes in each layer, and what's the dependency direction?
model_answer_key_points: Reset / normalize layer (browser baseline, zero opinion)
Tokens layer (CSS custom properties — primitive then semantic — single source of truth, no selectors)
Primitives layer (un-styled or minimally-styled element bases: button, input, dialog — accept tokens)
Components layer (composite UI: Card, Modal, Toast — built on primitives, accept variants via tokens or props)
Patterns / recipes layer (composed app-level UI: SearchHeader, NavBar — live in product apps, can override tokens)
Utilities layer (functional one-offs — last in cascade order)
Dependency direction: outer layers consume inner; never the reverse. Tokens know nothing about components; components know nothing about apps.
Use @layer to make this declarative + cascade-predictable. Each layer is a published surface area; breaking changes are versioned.

questions.question: You inherit a 5-year-old Angular app with 4000+ Sass files, deep nesting, hard-coded colors, magic numbers, no tokens. Leadership wants a modern themable system without a 6-month freeze. What's your migration strategy?
model_answer_key_points: Audit first: build a script to extract all color literals, font sizes, spacing values — produce a frequency report
Tokenize the high-frequency 20% values first (covers 80% of usage)
Introduce a tokens layer as CSS custom properties — additive, doesn't break existing styles
Per-file migration: replace literals with var(--token-x) one PR at a time, gated by tests
Add a lint rule that disallows new color/spacing literals in modified files (ratchet pattern)
Theming becomes possible the moment tokens are in CSS vars — dark mode is the first proof point
Component-by-component refactor to primitives + composites where ROI is highest (button, input, modal first)
Tooling: capture-and-replace scripts, design-token integration via something like Style Dictionary or W3C DTCG
Communicate trade-offs: migration runs in parallel with feature work; no freeze required; measurable progress (% of files token-pure)

questions.question: Best practice for token tiers in a multi-brand design system?

options.text: Three tiers: primitive (raw values like blue-500), semantic (intent like color-action-primary), component (specific like button-bg). Components reference semantic; semantic references primitive; primitives are the leaves.
One flat list of color/spacing tokens; let component CSS decide everything else.
Components should reference primitives directly — fewer indirections, less to maintain.
Tier doesn't matter; structure tokens by team (auth-color-primary, billing-color-primary).

explanations.a: Correct. The three-tier model decouples meaning from value. Re-theming = swap the semantic→primitive bindings. Multi-brand = different semantic mappings, same component contracts.
explanations.b: Flat list works for tiny systems but breaks on theming and brand variants.
explanations.c: Wrong. Components binding directly to primitives means re-theming requires editing every component.
explanations.d: Wrong. Team-namespaced tokens reflect org structure, not design intent — and become legacy fast when teams reorg.

questions.question: What is the W3C DTCG (Design Tokens Community Group) format primarily standardizing?

options.text: A JSON schema for design tokens with `$value`, `$type`, references like `{color.brand.primary}`, plus extensions — designed as an interchange format between design tools and code transformers.
A CSS spec for native design tokens at runtime.
A Figma-only file format.
A binary token format optimized for bundle size.

explanations.a: Correct. DTCG is a vendor-neutral JSON spec for tokens that tools (Style Dictionary, Token Studio, design platforms) can read and emit. Solves the cross-tool interop problem.
explanations.b: Wrong. There's no native browser token spec; CSS custom properties are the runtime mechanism.

questions.question: Best runtime mechanism for theming a token-based design system (light/dark/high-contrast)?

options.text: CSS custom properties redefined under a [data-theme] attribute (or :root with prefers-color-scheme). Components consume var(--…) and don't know which theme is active.
Rebuild and swap a CSS bundle per theme at deploy time.
Inline all theme styles per component and toggle class names on every element.
Use JavaScript to recompute every style on theme change.

explanations.a: Correct. The whole point of token indirection. Components reference var(--color-surface); themes redefine the variable on a parent. Instant runtime switching, no rebuilds, no JS recomputation.
explanations.b: Possible but wasteful; modern systems use CSS vars.
explanations.c: Doesn't scale; defeats the value of tokenization.
explanations.d: Wrong. CSS variables already do this at the engine level — no JS needed.

questions.question: Design the pipeline: Figma → tokens → CSS custom properties → component library → product apps. Cover the seams.
model_answer_key_points: Source of truth: Figma variables exported via plugin (Tokens Studio / Variables2Code) to DTCG JSON
Tokens repo (versioned, semver) — runs transforms (Style Dictionary or custom) to emit CSS vars + SCSS maps + TS types
Component library imports tokens as CSS vars; never inlines values; published with peer-dep on token version
Product apps consume both component library and tokens; can override tokens per-app for theming
CI: any change to Figma variables that triggers token-repo emit must run visual regression on the component library before publish
Breaking change detection: name change or removed token = major bump
Doc surface: auto-generated token catalog page (per category, with live preview); component docs reference tokens
Tradeoff to flag: how often Figma syncs (manual vs scheduled vs on-edit) — manual = stale; on-edit = noisy; scheduled hourly is the usual compromise

questions.question: You're tokenizing typography for a responsive design system. Designers want T-shirt-size scales that fluidly adapt between breakpoints. Best token shape?

options.text: Each font-size token is a `clamp(min, preferred-with-vw, max)` expression — fluid by default, no media-query forking required.
Three separate tokens per size (mobile, tablet, desktop), components pick via media queries.
One size per scale; designers handle responsive in components.
Compute font sizes in JS based on viewport.resize events.

explanations.a: Correct. clamp() with viewport-relative middle term gives fluid type with explicit min/max. Cleanest in tokens — components reference one variable, fluidity is intrinsic.
explanations.b: Works but multiplies token count and forces every component to handle the responsive switch.
explanations.c: Pushes responsive concerns into components — defeats the point of tokens.
explanations.d: Wrong. JS-driven type is jittery, blocks main thread, and breaks SSR.

questions.question: When does adding a semantic (alias) token layer between primitives and components become harmful instead of helpful?

options.text: When the alias is 1:1 with a primitive and adds no intent — color-text-primary = gray-900 with no other consumer. Adds indirection without leverage.
Aliases are always good; more indirection is always better.
Aliases only help in dark-mode systems.
Aliases harm performance because CSS vars are slow.

explanations.a: Correct. Aliases earn their keep when they encode an intent that could swap (color-action-primary = brand-500 now, brand-600 in v2). 1:1 aliases are dead weight that obscure the source of truth.
explanations.b: Wrong. Excess indirection adds cognitive cost and makes refactors harder.
explanations.c: Aliases help for any reskin or rebrand, not just dark mode.
explanations.d: Wrong. CSS var lookups are O(1) and engine-level fast.

questions.question: For a Senior UI role at a SaaS company shipping a multi-app suite, you must pick the styling strategy. Best framing for the trade-off between Tailwind, CSS Modules, and CSS-in-JS?

options.text: Pick by where authoring friction is most expensive: Tailwind for high-volume product code (fast, design-token-aligned), CSS Modules for component libraries (stable contracts), CSS-in-JS only if dynamic theming via JS is required — which it usually isn't with CSS vars.
Always CSS-in-JS — it's the most modern.
Always Tailwind — utilities scale infinitely.
Always vanilla CSS — least dependencies.

explanations.a: Correct. The honest answer is contextual. Tailwind wins on speed + token alignment in app code. CSS Modules give clean component-library APIs with no JS runtime. CSS-in-JS adds runtime cost (Emotion/Styled) — modern alternatives like vanilla-extract are zero-runtime but more constrained.
explanations.b: Wrong. CSS-in-JS runtime libs have measurable cost (hydration mismatches, bundle size, performance).
explanations.c: Utility-only works in apps but consumers of a component library don't want to wire up Tailwind to use your <Button>.
explanations.d: Vanilla works but loses the affordances each option offers.

questions.question: In a component library, you want consumers to be able to style your <Modal /> from outside without forking it. Best primitive?

options.text: Expose styling hooks via ::part() on a web component, or document a small set of CSS variables consumers can override (e.g. --modal-bg, --modal-radius). Avoid leaking internal class names as a public API.
Use !important on every internal style so consumers know not to touch.
Make every internal class globally available; document them as the styling API.
Don't allow customization; force consumers to fork.

explanations.a: Correct. CSS vars and ::part() are the design-language for styling APIs in 2024+. They expose intent (background, radius) without leaking implementation (class names that may change). Stable contract for consumers.
explanations.b: !important escalates a war you don't want.
explanations.c: Leaking internal class names locks you into them — refactoring breaks consumers silently.
explanations.d: Doesn't scale; consumers will fork anyway.

questions.question: Your component library injects 180kb of CSS, mostly unused on any given page. Best strategy to reduce CSS shipped on initial render?

options.text: Per-component code-split CSS (CSS Modules / scoped styles emit one file per component, loader includes only what the page imports). Critical CSS for above-the-fold inlined via build tooling.
Switch to inline <style> tags everywhere.
Concatenate everything into one larger file for fewer HTTP requests.
Use !important on every selector to defeat tree-shaking.

explanations.a: Correct. Code-split the CSS along the same boundaries as JS. Modern bundlers + CSS Modules + framework-aware tooling (Next, Angular CLI, Vite) handle this. Then inline critical CSS for the LCP-critical fold.
explanations.b: Inline styles don't cache and bloat HTML.
explanations.c: Counterproductive — ships unused CSS to every page.

questions.question: Design: your design system has a <Card /> that needs to look one way at <= 320px wide (column layout, smaller font) and another at >= 480px (row layout, larger font), independent of viewport. The same card can appear in a wide hero or a narrow sidebar. How do you build this?
model_answer_key_points: Container queries — set container-type: inline-size on a wrapper inside the card (or on the card itself if it accepts the container responsibility)
@container (max-width: 320px) { … } / @container (min-width: 480px) { … } to switch layout/typography
Tokens for the breakpoint values — don't hardcode 320/480; reference --card-breakpoint-compact, --card-breakpoint-expanded
Use container query units (cqi, cqw) for fluid type/spacing within the card
Fallback strategy: in browsers without container query support (rare in 2025+), default to the larger layout via @supports not (container-type: inline-size) — degrade gracefully
Avoid coupling to viewport media queries — card lives anywhere, viewport is irrelevant to its layout choice
Test in Storybook with viewport + container-width controls to verify both modes

questions.question: Walk me through a design system or component library you owned. Cover token structure, component contracts, theming model, and how teams consumed it.
questions.model_answer_structure: STAR with strong technical depth on each layer.
model_answer_key_points: Concrete project — name the scope (apps consuming it, # of components, # of teams)
Token tiers — primitive / semantic / component (or whatever you used); reason for the choice
Component contract surface — props vs CSS vars vs slots/parts; how consumers customized without forking
Theming model — light/dark/brand; mechanism (CSS vars under data-theme); runtime vs build-time
Documentation — how teams discovered + adopted (Storybook? Live playground? Token catalog?)
Adoption challenge — at least one real friction (resistance from a team, perf issue, migration debt) and how you handled it
Outcome — measurable adoption % or velocity metric
What you'd change with current knowledge (container queries, @layer, DTCG, etc.)

questions.question: Tell me about a large frontend migration you led — framework upgrade, CSS overhaul, jQuery→modern, AngularJS→Angular, etc. How did you sequence it and avoid a feature freeze?
questions.model_answer_structure: STAR — strong on the sequencing decisions and risk management.
model_answer_key_points: What you migrated from/to and why (forced by EOL, perf, team velocity, etc.)
Coexistence pattern — both old and new running side by side (route-by-route, component-by-component, ng-upgrade, etc.)
Migration ratchet — automated check that NEW code can't use the old pattern (lint, CI)
Risk management — flags, canary, rollback plan
Stakeholder communication — how you sold the cost to PMs / leadership without a freeze
Concrete metric — % migrated per week, bugs introduced, perf delta
What broke and what you learned
Avoid 'we rewrote everything in a hackathon' answers — interviewers want surgical, incremental, measurable

questions.question: Describe the most complex UI you've built. What made it complex — scale, performance, real-time data, multi-tenant theming, accessibility under constraint — and what trade-offs did you make?
questions.model_answer_structure: STAR. Lead with the dimension of complexity, not 'we built a dashboard'.
model_answer_key_points: Pick ONE clear axis of complexity (not 'all of them')
Concrete scale — # of widgets, data volume, # of concurrent users, # of tenants
Architecture decisions tied to the complexity — virtualization, OnPush, signals, worker offload, cache layer
Real trade-off you accepted — feature you cut, browser you dropped, design you simplified for perf
Measurable outcome — load time, INP, ship date, adoption
What you'd do differently today with newer tooling
Candidate projects to draw from: Tallam (dashboards), Truelogy, Romoz, ADI, design-system-infrastructure. Pick the one with the strongest 'one axis' story and metric attached.

questions.question: Your design system Card component has a border-radius set via CSS var, but in one consumer app it's getting overridden to 0. Both apps use the same component version. Where do you look first?

options.text: The consumer app is setting --card-radius: 0 on an ancestor — inherited CSS vars cascade. Check :root and any wrapper with the var redefined.
The fallback `8px` is ignored when --card-radius is undefined.
CSS vars don't work across Shadow DOM boundaries.
Tailwind's preflight is resetting border-radius globally.

explanations.a: Correct. CSS custom properties inherit. A wrapper or :root setting --card-radius: 0 cascades into the card. Inspect with DevTools — Computed tab shows where the var was resolved.
explanations.b: Wrong. The fallback is used exactly when the var is undefined.
explanations.c: CSS vars DO penetrate shadow DOM (they're inherited custom properties).
explanations.d: Preflight sets a default, but the card uses border-radius via a var — preflight doesn't touch consumer-scoped vars.

questions.question: In Angular 17.1+, you wrote `name = input.required<string>();` then read `name` in the template as `{{ name }}`. The template shows `[Object object]`. What's wrong?

options.text: input() returns an InputSignal — you must call it: `{{ name() }}`.
input.required doesn't exist; it's input.mandatory.
Templates can't render signals directly.
input() requires zoneless mode to be enabled.

explanations.a: Correct. input() returns a signal-like InputSignal. Reading without invocation gives you the signal object, not its value. Use {{ name() }} or [attr]="name()".
explanations.c: Templates render signals fine — when you invoke them.
explanations.d: Wrong. input() works in both modes.
